title: CUDA-Q Accelerates Quantum Workloads at GTC 2026 - NVIDIA Quantum
why:
- CUDA-Q enables GPU-accelerated simulation for quantum algorithm development, with teams such as CINECA and Kipu Quantum running large-scale quantum optimization simulations (e.g., a 43-qubit statevector simulation on 2,048 NVIDIA GPUs).
- CUDA-Q is advancing quantum-classical hybrid applications, such as Infleqtion's Q4Bio biomarker discovery project, which consumed 24,000 NVIDIA GPU-node-hours and combines quantum processors with GPUs for inference.
- The platform has accelerated key research in quantum error correction, with Edinburgh researchers achieving a 900x speedup in color code decoding using CUDA-Q on NVIDIA GPUs, and Alice & Bob reporting a 9.25x speedup for elevator code simulations.
- Multiple organizations have integrated CUDA-Q with their quantum platforms and tools (e.g., PsiQuantum, TII, Pasqal, Scaleway, memQ) to enable scalable, hybrid quantum-GPU supercomputing and distributed quantum application development.
- CUDA-Q is facilitating the convergence of AI and quantum computing, with projects such as Hiverge's AI platform (the Hive) generating quantum circuits from natural language and Quantinuum using AI for quantum algorithm discovery in diverse real-world applications.
- Quantum simulation workloads that previously required extensive CPU resources can now be drastically accelerated on NVIDIA GPUs—for example, OTI Lumionics reduced materials calculation from 109 hours (on CPUs) to 1.2 hours (on GPU).
